question: Define an Abelian group and state the four group axioms it must satisfy.
expectedPoints: A group is a set G with a binary operation that combines any two elements to form a third.
It must satisfy Closure: For all a, b in G, the result of the operation a • b is also in G.
It must satisfy Associativity: For all a, b, c in G, (a • b) • c = a • (b • c).
It must have an Identity element: There exists an element e in G such that for every element a in G, e • a = a • e = a.
Every element must have an Inverse: For each a in G, there exists an element b in G such that a • b = b • a = e.
An Abelian group is a group that also satisfies Commutativity: For all a, b in G, a • b = b • a.

question: Explain why the set of all integers Z forms a group under the operation of addition, but does not form a group under the operation of multiplication.
expectedPoints: Under addition (+): The integers are closed under addition.
The associative property holds for addition of integers.
The identity element is 0, since a + 0 = a for any integer a.
The inverse of any integer a is -a, since a + (-a) = 0.
Therefore, (Z, +) satisfies all group axioms and is a group.
Under multiplication (×): The identity element would be 1, since a × 1 = a.
However, the multiplicative inverse of most integers is not an integer (e.g., the inverse of 2 would be 1/2, which is not in Z).
Since not every element has a multiplicative inverse within the set, (Z, ×) is not a group.

question: What is the identity element for the group of non-zero real numbers under multiplication? What is the inverse of a general element a in this group?
expectedPoints: The identity element for the group (R\{0}, ×) is the number 1.
This is because for any non-zero real number a, a × 1 = 1 × a = a.
The inverse of a general element a is its multiplicative reciprocal, 1/a.
This is because a × (1/a) = (1/a) × a = 1, which is the identity element.
